notes.anchor: What letters were read out in the Senate on the Ides of April
notes.note: Idus Apriles = 13 April 43 BC; the sensational reading of letters in Brutus's and Antony's names touched off a constitutional crisis over Antony's self-styled proconsular authority.

notes.anchor: the commonwealth should be freed not from a king alone, but from the kingship
notes.note: Cicero distinguishes tyrannicidium (killing the tyrant) from abolishing the tyranny itself; his complaint is that sparing Antony and other Caesarians after the Ides of March preserved the structural conditions for renewed autocracy.

notes.anchor: some god had not put that mind into Caesar Octavianus
notes.note: A rare moment of explicit gratitude to Octavian (the future Augustus), whose private legions in autumn 44 BC gave the Senate a counterweight to Antony; Cicero here acknowledges divine fortune in Octavian's timely action.

A messenger of disputed loyalties who arrives at dawn on the Ides of April carrying letters purportedly from both Brutus and Antony; his willingness to deliver Antony's letter to a magistrate contrasted with the timidity of Dolabella's own couriers.

The title proconsul (procos.) was a claim to legitimate imperium over a province; Antony had issued this to himself after the Lex Antonia assigned him the Gallic provinces, but the Senate had not confirmed it — hence the astonishment.

notes.anchor: with what danger his own son, with what danger mine
notes.note: Sestius's son and Cicero's son (Marcus the younger) were both serving under Brutus; if Antony were recognized as proconsul, their bearing arms against him would legally constitute treason, making the constitutional question acutely personal.

The three brothers: Marcus Antonius (Mark Antony), Gaius Antonius (held by Brutus in Macedonia), and Lucius Antonius (tribune 44, consul 41 BC); Cicero argues that clemency toward any one of them undermines the condemnation of Dolabella.

notes.anchor: The 18th day before the kalends of May
notes.note: Ante diem duodevicesimum Kalendas Maias = 14 April 43 BC; this date formula confirms the letter was sent the day after the dramatic Senate session of the Ides of April.
